did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) trained with meta-heuristic optimization algorithms (specifically Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm-II, Particle Swarm Optimization, and Genetic Algorithm) to classify dermoscopic images of skin diseases (angioma, basal cell carcinoma, and lentigo simplex) from the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a meta-heuristic supported Artificial Neural Network (NN-NSGA-II) for skin disease classification. It details specific methods including feature extraction (PCA, GLCM), dataset usage (ISIC 2017), and comparative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure) against NN-GA and NN-PSO models, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Detection of Skin disease using Metaheuristic supported Artificial Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes a meta-heuristic supported Artificial Neural Network (NN-NSGA-II) for the automated classification of dermoscopic skin disease images. The research focuses on distinguishing three specific skin conditions: angioma, basal cell carcinoma, and lentigo simplex, using data from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset. Feature extraction was performed using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) techniques on wavelet-transformed images. The proposed NN-NSGA-II model was trained using the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm-II and compared against classifiers trained with Particle Swarm Optimization (NN-PSO) and Genetic Algorithm (NN-GA). Evaluation metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure demonstrated that the NN-NSGA-II model achieved superior performance with 87.92% accuracy, outperforming NN-PSO (82.42%) and NN-GA (72.02%).

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three common skin diseases considered for classification in the study, sourced from the ISIC dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin diseases (Angioma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Lentigo Simplex)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin disease images. The model predicts one of three discrete classes: angioma, basal cell carcinoma, or lentigo simplex. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure derived from a confusion matrix.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: Images obtained from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2017 challenge. The dataset contains dermoscopy images of different types of skin diseases, with clinical metadata associated by skin disease experts. Images vary in resolution (1022x767 to 6748x4499), angles, and lighting conditions, and some contain artifacts.

use.training: Used to train the Artificial Neural Networks (NN-NSGA-II, NN-PSO, NN-GA) for classification.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the classifiers using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between NN-NSGA-II, NN-PSO, and NN-GA were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure) without statistical significance analysis.

ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset.experiment: Comparison of NN-NSGA-II, NN-PSO, and NN-GA for classification of Angioma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Lentigo Simplex
ISIC 2017 Challenge Dataset.notes: The NN-NSGA-II model achieved superior performance compared to NN-PSO and NN-GA. The paper notes that NN-GA was poor at detecting the skin diseases under study despite high precision, while NN-PSO showed significant improvement. NN-NSGA-II is concluded to give better results.

architecture_used.main_model: Artificial Neural Network (ANN) trained with Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm-II (NN-NSGA-II), Particle Swarm Optimization (NN-PSO), and Genetic Algorithm (NN-GA)

architecture_details.network_structure: Artificial Neural Network with one hidden layer
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Single-level discrete 2-D wavelet transform (3 times) using Daubechies DB4 filter on binary image, followed by Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on approximation and detail coefficients, then Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) computation

training_details.objective_function: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for single objective optimization; RMSE along with Maximum error for multi-objective optimization
training_details.selection_method: Binary tournament method with non-dominated sorting

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: NN-NSGA-II (Artificial Neural Network trained with Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm-II)
NN-PSO (Artificial Neural Network trained with Particle Swarm Optimization)
NN-GA (Artificial Neural Network trained with Genetic Algorithm)
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of original images to binary images
Application of Single-level discrete 2-D wavelet transform (3 times) using Daubechies DB4 filter on binary images
Application of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) on approximation and detail coefficients
Computation of Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) from PCA matrices
conclusions: The proposed NN-NSGA-II model, which uses Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm-II to train an Artificial Neural Network, achieved superior performance compared to NN-PSO and NN-GA for classifying angioma, basal cell carcinoma, and lentigo simplex.
The NN-NSGA-II model achieved an accuracy of 87.92%, precision of 94.2%, recall of 87.5%, and F-measure of 90.73%.
The NN-GA classifier performed poorly with 72.02% accuracy, despite having a high precision of 93.6%, indicating it was poor at detecting the skin diseases under study.
The NN-PSO classifier showed significant improvement over NN-GA with 82.42% accuracy, 93.8% precision, 81.54% recall, and 87.24% F-measure.
NSGA II trained ANN gives better results than other meta-heuristic methods (PSO and GA) for this specific dermoscopic image classification task.
The authors suggest that applying the same approach on a larger training data set could potentially lead to a better predictive model.
important_remarks: The study is limited to only three specific skin diseases (angioma, basal cell carcinoma, and lentigo simplex), excluding malignant melanoma and other common skin conditions, which limits the generalizability of the findings.

The paper does not report the specific number of images used for training, validation, or testing, nor does it provide a breakdown of class distribution, raising concerns about potential dataset bias or class imbalance.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the NN-NSGA-II model over NN-PSO and NN-GA; comparisons rely solely on descriptive metrics.

The dataset contains images with varying resolutions (1022x767 to 6748x4499), angles, lighting conditions, and artifacts, which may introduce variability and affect model robustness not fully addressed in the methodology.

The authors acknowledge that the approach was tested on a limited dataset and suggest that applying the same method to a larger training dataset could lead to better predictive models, indicating current results may be preliminary.

The NN-GA model exhibited poor recall (61.91%) despite high precision (93.6%), indicating a significant false-negative rate for detecting the target skin diseases, which is a critical clinical limitation.
The paper does not discuss external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of the model's generalization capability beyond the specific ISIC 2017 challenge subset used.
